It's that heavy feeling you get when people expect you to fail because of who you are, and that expectation somehow gets into your head and makes things harder. For me, being a woman in mechanics for decades here in Bari, I've felt those looks and heard those comments more times than I can count. When I started working, people would assume I couldn't handle the technical aspects, that maybe I was just there to answer phones or clean up. That doubt from others creates doubt in yourself, even when you know what you're doing.

Should we address it? Absolutely, but I worry sometimes about how. I've raised five children and worked full-time my whole adult life, and I've seen how these expectations damage young people especially. My parents had good education, my father even got a master's degree, but I only finished primary school. Sometimes I wonder if I'd internalized some of those stereotypes myself, thinking technical work wasn't for someone like me to study formally, even though I had the curiosity and the hands to do the work itself.

The way forward isn't simple, and honestly, it makes me anxious thinking about all the layers involved. From what I see on social media where I spend quite a bit of time these days, there's a lot of talk but not always practical action. I think we need to start young, showing children that anyone can work with engines, computers, or science equipment regardless of their gender or background. In workshops and schools, we should have diverse role models visible and present. When I occasionally mentor younger mechanics, I make sure to be patient and thorough, showing them that competence isn't about fitting a stereotype but about dedication and skill.

We also need workplaces to actively counter these biases, not just talk about them. Managers should evaluate work fairly, without assumptions. It requires conscious effort and accountability. As someone who values structure and doing things properly, I believe systematic approaches work better than just hoping attitudes change. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Stereotype threat in STEM is something I've lived through my entire working life, though I didn't always have a name for it. It's that heavy feeling you get when people expect you to fail because of who you are, and that expectation somehow gets into your head and makes things harder. For me, being a woman in mechanics for decades here in Bari, I've felt those looks and heard those comments more times than I can count. When I started working, people would assume I couldn't handle the technical aspects, that maybe I was just there to answer phones or clean up. That doubt from others creates doubt in yourself, even when you know what you're doing.

Should we address it? Absolutely, but I worry sometimes about how. I've raised five children and worked full-time my whole adult life, and I've seen how these expectations damage young people especially. My parents had good education, my father even got a master's degree, but I only finished primary school. Sometimes I wonder if I'd internalized some of those stereotypes myself, thinking technical work wasn't for someone like me to study formally, even though I had the curiosity and the hands to do the work itself.

The way forward isn't simple, and honestly, it makes me anxious thinking about all the layers involved. From what I see on social media where I spend quite a bit of time these days, there's a lot of talk but not always practical action. I think we need to start young, showing children that anyone can work with engines, computers, or science equipment regardless of their gender or background. In workshops and schools, we should have diverse role models visible and present. When I occasionally mentor younger mechanics, I make sure to be patient and thorough, showing them that competence isn't about fitting a stereotype but about dedication and skill.

We also need workplaces to actively counter these biases, not just talk about them. Managers should evaluate work fairly, without assumptions. It requires conscious effort and accountability. As someone who values structure and doing things properly, I believe systematic approaches work better than just hoping attitudes change. It's uncomfortable work, and change feels slow, but my faith teaches me that perseverance in doing what's right matters, even when it's difficult.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 62-year-old genderqueer mechanic with primary education who has worked in a male-dominated field for decades, providing direct lived experience with stereotype threat. High conscientiousness suggests structured, thorough thinking. High neuroticism indicates anxiety about complex issues and worry about outcomes. Low extraversion means a more reserved, introspective approach. Moderate openness suggests practical rather than highly abstract thinking. Curiosity drives engagement with the topic. Being a parent of five children provides perspective on youth development. Limited formal education but high parental education creates interesting tension about internalized stereotypes. Christian faith provides moral framework. Social media use means exposure to contemporary discussions. The tone should be personal, reflective, somewhat anxious but determined, grounded in practical experience rather than academic theory.
response_parsed.tone: Personal, reflective, somewhat anxious yet determined, grounded in practical lived experience, introspective, conscientious, moderately cautious about change while recognizing its necessity
